_notes: Keyed by a short operator code (your own convention — GWR, AWC, etc). 2026-07-13: seeded 28 new entries from api/incidents.js's TOC_REGION_TABLE, using that table's own 2-letter ATOC-style codes (VT, GR, XC, ...) as the key — name only, awaiting Wikipedia/manual enrichment like any other entry. RESOLVED: WMR (West Midlands Railway) predates that seed and keeps its own, longer code on purpose — TOC_REGION_TABLE's WM is a real-world ATOC operator code required for matching the live Darwin incident feed in api/incidents.js, not an arbitrary internal choice, so it is NOT changed to WMR for internal consistency; do not add a WM entry here. 2026-07-13 canonicalization pass (see chat): `aliases` (array of strings) is a new manual/curated field — OSM-tagged naming variants (spelling, casing, historical franchise/legal names) that should be treated as the same operator as this entry's `name`; also added to matching stations-content.json entries' `operators` arrays, replacing the variant string with the canonical `name`. `legal_entity` (string) is a separate new manual/curated field for a DIFFERENT real entity behind a brand (e.g. the group company operating it) — deliberately NOT folded into `aliases`/station data, since equating a legal entity with its brand would be wrong, not just imprecise. 2026-07-14: SE (Southeastern)'s wikipedia_title set to "Southeastern (train operating company)" — verified live against Wikipedia's API as a real, non-disambiguation page, exact match. GN (Great Northern)'s wikipedia_title set to "Greater Thameslink Railway", NOT its own article — bare "Great Northern" is a disambiguation page and no "Great Northern (train operating company)" article exists; Wikipedia confirms Thameslink Southern Great Northern Ltd, trading as Greater Thameslink Railway (GTR), took over Govia Thameslink Railway's services on 31 May 2026 and runs Gatwick Express/Great Northern/Southern/Thameslink as sub-brands with no separate per-brand articles post-merger, so GTR's article is genuinely the correct/only source for Great Northern content today. This is a deliberate exception to the usual "wikipedia_title points to the entity's own article" rule — flagged here so it isn't mistaken for a mis-resolution later. 2026-07-14 follow-up: swept SN/TL/GX/GN (the four Greater Thameslink Railway sub-brands) for stale "Govia Thameslink Railway" references. SN's headline/parent_company updated to "Greater Thameslink Railway". TL and GX already had it correctly recorded as history (a franchises entry ending 31 May 2026, TL also has the new "Greater Thameslink Railway" entry starting then) — left untouched, that's accurate history not a stale current-parent field. GN had no Govia reference to begin with (freshly set this session). NOTE left unresolved: TL's parent_company is "Govia" (not "Govia Thameslink Railway") — a different, narrower string referring to the private-sector parent group; didn't touch it since it wasn't the string asked about, but it's arguably equally stale post-nationalization and may need its own follow-up. 2026-07-14: "Southern Railway" folded into SN/Southern as a 19th alias (added to its `aliases` array; 15 stations' operators arrays updated) — after the membership-checking extraction switch this string reappeared as a genuinely stop-verified (not proximity) OSM tag, always co-occurring with "Southern" at the same stations, confirming it's a stale/duplicate relation tag for the same brand rather than the historic 1923–1947 Southern Railway company or proximity noise. By contrast "Greater Thameslink Railway" (which also reappeared under membership-checking, co-occurring with Thameslink/Southern/Gatwick Express/Great Northern) was deliberately NOT folded anywhere — it's the real current legal successor entity (GTR, since 31 May 2026) that Southern/Thameslink/Great Northern/Gatwick Express now operate under as sub-brands, confirmed via its own Wikipedia article; equating it with any one sub-brand would be wrong, same reasoning as `legal_entity`. fleet_classes store the EXACT display label as it should appear (not a bare number + assumed 'Class ' prefix) — this covers cases like Manchester Metrolink's M5000 that don't follow the 'Class NN' pattern. 'id' is used to link through to that vehicle's entry on the database page; 'display' is shown verbatim, no reformatting. franchises is the full history; sidebar should show the current one as a simple 'Operating since {start}' line by default, with the full array available as an expandable detail. FIELD OWNERSHIP (see scripts/ for the population scripts): headline/parent_company/franchises are written by scripts/fetch-wikipedia-facts.mjs (Wikipedia, narrative/historical — Claude extracts, never invents; headline is a punchy 8-12 word sentence, not a paragraph; a franchise entry only appears if the article states its dates). schema_jsonld is written by scripts/build-schema-jsonld.mjs (template assembly from the fields above, no new facts, no API calls). stations_operated/regions_served/fleet_classes are manual/curated only — neither OSM nor Wikipedia reliably has these without real risk of being wrong, so they're deliberately not automated. name/wikipedia_title are manual/curated only. _wikipedia is provenance metadata written by the Wikipedia script (no _osm block here — OSM doesn't own any operator-level field in this design; the OSM-derived operator name lives on the station/route entries that reference it, via their own operator/operators fields). 2026-07-13 follow-up: `sub_brands` (array of strings, on LE/Greater Anglia so far) is a named service brand under this operator that intentionally does NOT get its own operators-content.json entry and is NOT folded into `aliases`/station data — distinct from both a naming variant (aliases) and a different legal entity (legal_entity). `welsh_name` (string, on AW/Transport for Wales so far) is the official bilingual Welsh-language name — kept as its own field rather than merged into `aliases` because it's a live, still-used official name, not a deprecated/historical variant; station-content.json's "Trafnidiaeth Cymru" tags are deliberately left as-is, not replaced with "Transport for Wales".
